# Figure description: 4_Карта_прогнозов_N-42-XXXIII.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **forecast/predictive geological map** (*карта прогнозов*) with elements showing the spatial regularities of mineral deposit distribution. It is a thematic overlay map compiled at 1:200,000 scale, combining geological mapping with mineral prospectivity assessment. The map appears to be a hand-drafted/printed document typical of Soviet-era geological survey outputs.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The map covers sheet **М-42-XXXIII** of the standard Soviet topographic/geological grid system (1:200,000 scale), located in **northern Kazakhstan**. The map was compiled in **1984** as part of the Central Kazakhstan Production Geological Association's regional survey program (Report No. 36572). The sheet covers a portion of the region surveyed under the broader M-42/N-42 program. The map is stamped with the **Kazakh SSR Republican Geological Fund** (*Казахский ССР Республиканский геологический фонд*), inventory number **36572**.

---

## Key Content

The map displays a complex geological setting with the following key features:

**Geological Units:**
- **KZ** — widespread label across the map, likely denoting **Cenozoic** cover deposits (Quaternary-Neogene sediments), which dominate large portions of the map area, particularly in the northern and eastern sectors.
- **Г** (Г) — appears in several locations, likely denoting **granite** intrusive bodies or a specific stratigraphic/lithological unit.
- **PAr / Rаr** — labels suggesting **Proterozoic (Riphean) or Archean** metamorphic basement rocks, concentrated in the central and southeastern portions of the map.
- **C₁, C₂** — likely **Lower and Upper Carboniferous** units, visible in the western/southwestern inset area.
- **D** — possibly **Devonian** formations.
- **Рφ** or similar symbols — likely **Paleozoic** intrusive or volcanic units.

**Structural Features:**
- Prominent **linear fault zones** are shown as bold diagonal lines crossing the central portion of the map from northwest to southeast, with associated dashed lines suggesting subsidiary or inferred faults.
- **Concentric/elliptical contours** in the lower-left inset area strongly suggest a **ring structure** or **dome structure**, possibly a buried intrusive body or a structural dome with radiating fault patterns — a classic target for ore deposit prospectivity in Soviet geological methodology.
- **Hatched (red/pink diagonal lines)** in the upper-left portion of the main map panel likely indicate a **prospective or anomalous zone**, possibly a geophysical anomaly area or a restricted/intensely studied zone.

**Mineral Occurrence Symbols:**
- **Solid black circles (●)** — cluster notably in the central portion of the map (around the "800" and "1000" contour labels), representing **known mineral occurrences or deposits**. There is a notable concentration of these symbols in two clusters, suggesting a mineralized district or ore field.
- **Green-colored irregular outlines/patches** — scattered across the map, likely representing **zones of hydrothermal alteration, skarn, or mineralized bodies** (a common convention in Soviet ore geology maps). These are concentrated along structural corridors.
- **Dashed curved lines** — contour lines possibly representing **depth to basement**, **geophysical isolines** (gravity or magnetic), or **structural contours**. Values of **800** and **1000** are visible, likely in meters (depth or elevation).

**Inset Panel (lower left):**
- A separate inset map panel shows a structurally complex area with the prominent **ring/dome structure** mentioned above, with geological unit labels (C₁, C₂, KZ, PAr, Г) and additional mineral occurrence symbols. This inset likely represents a **detailed view of a specific prospective area** within the sheet.

## Research Value

This forecast map synthesizes the geological, structural, and mineralogical data for sheet M-42-XXXIII to delineate **prospective zones for mineral deposits** — most likely **polymetallic, copper, or gold mineralization** given the Carboniferous-Devonian volcanic-plutonic associations and the style of green-highlighted hydrothermal zones. The clustering of mineral occurrence symbols along fault intersections and around the ring structure provides a spatial model for **ore-controlling structures**, which is directly relevant to targeting future exploration drilling. For an AI research assistant, this map answers the question: *"Where are the highest-priority areas for mineral exploration on this sheet, and what geological controls govern their distribution?"* — making it a key prospectivity synthesis document within the
